heading: 5.5.3.Tiled Mode
content:
This section talks about how Tensor and Tensor access work in tiled mode.

#### 5.5.3.1. [Bounding Box](#tensor-tiled-mode-bounding-box)

A tensor can be accessed in chunks known as *Bounding Box*. The Bounding Box has the same
dimensionality as the tensor they are accessing into. Size of each bounding Box must be a multiple
of 16 bytes. The address of the bounding Box must also be aligned to 16 bytes.

Bounding Box has the following access properties:

* Bounding Box dimension sizes
* Out of boundary access mode
* Traversal strides

The tensor-coordinates, specified in the PTX tensor instructions, specify the starting offset of the
bounding box. Starting offset of the bounding box along with the rest of the bounding box
information together are used to determine the elements which are to be accessed.

#### 5.5.3.2. [Traversal-Stride](#tensor-tiled-mode-traversal-stride)

While the Bounding Box is iterating the tensor across a dimension, the traversal stride specifies
the exact number of elements to be skipped. If no jump over is required, default value of 1 must be
specified.

The traversal stride in dimension 0 can be used for the [Interleave layout](#tensor-interleaved-layout).
For non-interleaved layout, the traversal stride in
dimension 0 must always be 1.

[Figure 8](#tensor-tiled-mode-bb-example) illustrates tensor, tensor size, tensor stride,
Bounding Box size and traversal stride.





[Figure: tensor-tiled-mode-bounding-box-example]
FIGURE DESCRIPTION: Tiled Mode Bounding Box, Tensor Size and Traversal Stride

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the spatial relationships between several key parameters used in NVIDIA PTX ISA tiled memory access mode. It shows a 3D tensor (represented as a large wireframe cube) containing a smaller solid 3D block (the tensor data region), which in turn contains an even smaller access box (shown in green). The figure demonstrates how AccessBoxsize, TraversalStride, TensorSize, and TensorStride relate to each other in both dimensions 0 (horizontal/X) and 1 (vertical/Y).

THREE-DIMENSIONAL STRUCTURE:
1. OUTERMOST STRUCTURE - Large wireframe cube (white/outline only): Represents the full bounding region or memory space. This is the largest structure in the figure.

2. MIDDLE STRUCTURE - Solid 3D block (blue/dark blue): Represents the actual tensor data. It is positioned inside the wireframe cube, offset toward the front-left. The front face of this block is a grid of cells (approximately 9 columns wide by 9 rows tall visible on the front face), rendered in dark blue. The top face is rendered in lighter blue.

3. INNERMOST STRUCTURE - Green highlighted region: Located in the lower-left portion of the front face of the blue tensor block. This green region represents the AccessBox (the actual data access window). It spans approximately 6 columns wide by 4 rows tall on the front face of the tensor.

LABELED DIMENSIONS (all labels are in blue text with blue arrows):

DIMENSION 0 (horizontal, shown along the bottom of the figure, left-to-right arrows):
- AccessBoxsize[0]: Horizontal width of the green access box region. Arrow spans the width of the green region along the bottom edge of the tensor front face.
- TraversalStride[0]: Horizontal traversal stride. Arrow is slightly wider than AccessBoxsize[0], shown just below the tensor.
- TensorSize[0]: Width of the entire tensor (blue block) in dimension 0. Arrow spans the full width of the blue tensor block, shown below TraversalStride[0].
- TensorStride[0]: The largest horizontal measurement shown. Arrow spans beyond TensorSize[0], shown at the very bottom of the figure, indicating the stride in memory between rows of the tensor (includes padding).

DIMENSION 1 (vertical, shown along the left side of the figure, up-down arrows):
- AccessBoxsize[1]: Vertical height of the green access box. Small double-headed arrow on the left side of the tensor, spanning the height of the green region.
- TraversalStride[1]: Vertical traversal stride. Double-headed arrow slightly taller than AccessBoxsize[1], shown to the left of AccessBoxsize[1].
- TensorSize[1]: Height of the entire tensor in dimension 1. Double-headed arrow spanning the full height of the blue tensor block, shown further to the left.
- TensorStride[1]: The largest vertical measurement. Double-headed arrow spanning beyond TensorSize[1], shown at the far left of the figure, indicating the stride in memory between planes/slices of the tensor.

SPATIAL RELATIONSHIPS AND HIERARCHY:
- AccessBoxsize[0] <= TraversalStride[0] <= TensorSize[0] <= TensorStride[0]
- AccessBoxsize[1] <= TraversalStride[1] <= TensorSize[1] <= TensorStride[1]
- The AccessBox (green) fits within the Tensor (blue block), which fits within the overall bounding box (wireframe cube).
- TraversalStride defines the step size between successive tile positions during traversal.
- TensorStride is larger than TensorSize, indicating that there may be padding between rows/planes in memory.
- The wireframe outer cube represents the full memory bounding box that contains the tensor.

GRID DETAIL:
The front face of the blue tensor block is divided into a regular grid of cells (approximately 9x9 cells visible). The green access box covers the lower-left portion of this grid (approximately 6 columns by 4 rows), clearly showing that the access box is a sub-region of the full tensor face.

COLOR CODING:
- White/outline: Outer bounding box (memory region)
- Dark blue (front face grid): Tensor data region
- Light blue (top face): Top surface of tensor
- Green: AccessBox region (the active data access window)
- Blue arrows and text: All dimension labels and measurements

FIGURE DESCRIPTION: Tiled Mode Bounding Box, Tensor Size and Traversal Stride

CONCEPT OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the spatial relationships between several key parameters used in NVIDIA PTX ISA tiled memory access mode. It shows a 3D tensor (represented as a large wireframe cube) containing a smaller solid 3D block (the tensor data region), which in turn contains an even smaller access box (shown in green). The figure demonstrates how AccessBoxsize, TraversalStride, TensorSize, and TensorStride relate to each other in both dimensions 0 (horizontal/X) and 1 (vertical/Y).

THREE-DIMENSIONAL STRUCTURE:
1. OUTERMOST STRUCTURE - Large wireframe cube (white/outline only): Represents the full bounding region or memory space. This is the largest structure in the figure.

2. MIDDLE STRUCTURE - Solid 3D block (blue/dark blue): Represents the actual tensor data. It is positioned inside the wireframe cube, offset toward the front-left. The front face of this block is a grid of cells (approximately 9 columns wide by 9 rows tall visible on the front face), rendered in dark blue. The top face is rendered in lighter blue.

3. INNERMOST STRUCTURE - Green highlighted region: Located in the lower-left portion of the front face of the blue tensor block. This green region represents the AccessBox (the actual data access window). It spans approximately 6 columns wide by 4 rows tall on the front face of the tensor.

LABELED DIMENSIONS (all labels are in blue text with blue arrows):

DIMENSION 0 (horizontal, shown along the bottom of the figure, left-to-right arrows):
- AccessBoxsize[0]: Horizontal width of the green access box region. Arrow spans the width of the green region along the bottom edge of the tensor front face.
- TraversalStride[0]: Horizontal traversal stride. Arrow is slightly wider than AccessBoxsize[0], shown just below the tensor.
- TensorSize[0]: Width of the entire tensor (blue block) in dimension 0. Arrow spans the full width of the blue tensor block, shown below TraversalStride[0].
- TensorStride[0]: The largest horizontal measurement shown. Arrow spans beyond TensorSize[0], shown at the very bottom of the figure, indicating the stride in memory between rows of the tensor (includes padding).

DIMENSION 1 (vertical, shown along the left side of the figure, up-down arrows):
- AccessBoxsize[1]: Vertical height of the green access box. Small double-headed arrow on the left side of the tensor, spanning the height of the green region.
- TraversalStride[1]: Vertical traversal stride. Double-headed arrow slightly taller than AccessBoxsize[1], shown to the left of AccessBoxsize[1].
- TensorSize[1]: Height of the entire tensor in dimension 1. Double-headed arrow spanning the full height of the blue tensor block, shown further to the left.
- TensorStride[1]: The largest vertical measurement. Double-headed arrow spanning beyond TensorSize[1], shown at the far left of the figure, indicating the stride in memory between planes/slices of the tensor.

SPATIAL RELATIONSHIPS AND HIERARCHY:
- AccessBoxsize[0] <= TraversalStride[0] <= TensorSize[0] <= TensorStride[0]
- AccessBoxsize[1] <= TraversalStride[1] <= TensorSize[1] <= TensorStride[1]
- The AccessBox (green) fits within the Tensor (blue block), which fits within the overall bounding box (wireframe cube).
- TraversalStride defines the step size between successive tile positions during traversal.
- TensorStride is larger than TensorSize, indicating that there may be padding between rows/planes in memory.
- The wireframe outer cube represents the full memory bounding box that contains the tensor.

GRID DETAIL:
The front face of the blue tensor block is divided into a regular grid of cells (approximately 9x9 cells visible). The green access box covers the lower-left portion of this grid (approximately 6 columns by 4 rows), clearly showing that the access box is a sub-region of the full tensor face.

COLOR CODING:
- White/outline: Outer bounding box (memory region)
- Dark blue (front face grid): Tensor data region
- Light blue (top face): Top surface of tensor
- Green: AccessBox region (the active data access window)
- Blue arrows and text: All dimension labels and measurements


Figure 8 Tiled mode bounding box, tensor size and traversal stride

#### 5.5.3.3. [Out of Boundary Access](#tensor-tiled-mode-oob-access)

PTX Tensor operation can detect and handle the case when the Bounding Box crosses the tensor
boundary in any dimension. There are 2 modes:

* Zero fill mode:

  Elements in the Bounding Box which fall outside of the tensor boundary are set to 0.
* `OOB-NaN` fill mode:

  Elements in the Bounding Box which fall outside of the tensor boundary are set to a special NaN
  called `OOB-NaN`.

[Figure 9](#tensor-oob-access) shows an example of the out of boundary access.





[Figure: tensor-oob-access]
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates the concept of "out of boundary access" in the context of texture or surface memory access in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows what happens when memory access blocks (representing texture fetch regions or load/store access windows) extend beyond the boundaries of a defined memory region (such as a texture or surface).

COMPONENTS AND THEIR RELATIONSHIPS:

1. GREEN RECTANGLE (large, central):
   - Represents the valid/defined memory region, such as a texture surface or buffer.
   - It is the largest element in the figure, occupying the central area.
   - This is the "in-bounds" region of memory.

2. BLUE RECTANGLES (labeled "access block"):
   - There are five blue rectangles total in the figure.
   - They represent the access blocks — the regions being accessed by memory operations (e.g., texture fetches or surface loads).
   - Each blue rectangle represents a single access operation's footprint.
   - Positions of the blue rectangles:
     a. Upper-center: partially inside the green region, partially extending above the top edge of the green region.
     b. Upper-right: partially inside the green region, partially extending above and to the right of the green region's top-right corner.
     c. Left-center: partially inside the green region, partially extending to the left of the green region's left edge.
     d. Center: fully inside the green region (entirely within bounds — no out-of-bound fill visible).
     e. Lower-right: partially inside the green region, partially extending to the right and below the green region's right/bottom-right edge.
   - The lower-right blue rectangle is specifically annotated with the label "access block" via an arrow.

3. YELLOW/ORANGE RECTANGLES (labeled "out-of-bound fill"):
   - There are yellow/orange regions visible at the edges where the blue access blocks extend beyond the green region.
   - They represent the portions of the access block that fall outside the valid memory boundary — i.e., the out-of-bounds area.
   - The yellow regions appear as the parts of the access block footprint that lie outside the green rectangle.
   - Positions of yellow out-of-bound fill regions:
     a. Upper-center: yellow region above the top edge of the green rectangle, corresponding to the top portion of the upper-center blue block.
     b. Upper-right: yellow region above and to the right of the green rectangle's top-right corner, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the upper-right blue block.
     c. Left-center: yellow region to the left of the green rectangle's left edge, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the left-center blue block. There appear to be two yellow strips on the left side (top and bottom of the left-extending blue block).
     d. Lower-right: yellow region to the right of and/or below the green rectangle's right edge, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the lower-right blue block.
   - The lower-right yellow region is specifically annotated with the label "out-of-bound fill" via an arrow.

ANNOTATIONS AND LABELS:
- "access block" — text label on the right side of the figure, with an arrow pointing to the lower-right blue rectangle. Indicates that blue rectangles represent access blocks.
- "out-of-bound fill" — text label on the right side of the figure, with an arrow pointing to the lower-right yellow/orange region. Indicates that yellow/orange regions represent the out-of-bounds fill values returned or used when an access extends beyond the valid memory boundary.

NUMERICAL VALUES / DIMENSIONS:
- No explicit numerical dimensions or sizes are labeled in the figure. The sizes are illustrative/relative only.

DATA FLOW / ORGANIZATION PATTERN:
- The figure demonstrates that when an access block (blue) is positioned such that part of it overlaps with the valid memory region (green) and part of it falls outside, the out-of-bounds portion is filled with a designated "out-of-bound fill" value (yellow/orange).
- The one fully interior blue rectangle (center of the green region) has no yellow fill, confirming that fully in-bounds accesses produce no out-of-bound fill.
- The pattern shows four different boundary scenarios: top edge overflow, top-right corner overflow, left edge overflow, and right/bottom-right edge overflow.
- This is consistent with PTX texture/surface boundary handling modes where out-of-bounds accesses return a fill value rather than wrapping or clamping.

CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure illustrates the concept of "out of boundary access" in the context of texture or surface memory access in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows what happens when memory access blocks (representing texture fetch regions or load/store access windows) extend beyond the boundaries of a defined memory region (such as a texture or surface).

COMPONENTS AND THEIR RELATIONSHIPS:

1. GREEN RECTANGLE (large, central):
   - Represents the valid/defined memory region, such as a texture surface or buffer.
   - It is the largest element in the figure, occupying the central area.
   - This is the "in-bounds" region of memory.

2. BLUE RECTANGLES (labeled "access block"):
   - There are five blue rectangles total in the figure.
   - They represent the access blocks — the regions being accessed by memory operations (e.g., texture fetches or surface loads).
   - Each blue rectangle represents a single access operation's footprint.
   - Positions of the blue rectangles:
     a. Upper-center: partially inside the green region, partially extending above the top edge of the green region.
     b. Upper-right: partially inside the green region, partially extending above and to the right of the green region's top-right corner.
     c. Left-center: partially inside the green region, partially extending to the left of the green region's left edge.
     d. Center: fully inside the green region (entirely within bounds — no out-of-bound fill visible).
     e. Lower-right: partially inside the green region, partially extending to the right and below the green region's right/bottom-right edge.
   - The lower-right blue rectangle is specifically annotated with the label "access block" via an arrow.

3. YELLOW/ORANGE RECTANGLES (labeled "out-of-bound fill"):
   - There are yellow/orange regions visible at the edges where the blue access blocks extend beyond the green region.
   - They represent the portions of the access block that fall outside the valid memory boundary — i.e., the out-of-bounds area.
   - The yellow regions appear as the parts of the access block footprint that lie outside the green rectangle.
   - Positions of yellow out-of-bound fill regions:
     a. Upper-center: yellow region above the top edge of the green rectangle, corresponding to the top portion of the upper-center blue block.
     b. Upper-right: yellow region above and to the right of the green rectangle's top-right corner, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the upper-right blue block.
     c. Left-center: yellow region to the left of the green rectangle's left edge, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the left-center blue block. There appear to be two yellow strips on the left side (top and bottom of the left-extending blue block).
     d. Lower-right: yellow region to the right of and/or below the green rectangle's right edge, corresponding to the out-of-bounds portion of the lower-right blue block.
   - The lower-right yellow region is specifically annotated with the label "out-of-bound fill" via an arrow.

ANNOTATIONS AND LABELS:
- "access block" — text label on the right side of the figure, with an arrow pointing to the lower-right blue rectangle. Indicates that blue rectangles represent access blocks.
- "out-of-bound fill" — text label on the right side of the figure, with an arrow pointing to the lower-right yellow/orange region. Indicates that yellow/orange regions represent the out-of-bounds fill values returned or used when an access extends beyond the valid memory boundary.

NUMERICAL VALUES / DIMENSIONS:
- No explicit numerical dimensions or sizes are labeled in the figure. The sizes are illustrative/relative only.

DATA FLOW / ORGANIZATION PATTERN:
- The figure demonstrates that when an access block (blue) is positioned such that part of it overlaps with the valid memory region (green) and part of it falls outside, the out-of-bounds portion is filled with a designated "out-of-bound fill" value (yellow/orange).
- The one fully interior blue rectangle (center of the green region) has no yellow fill, confirming that fully in-bounds accesses produce no out-of-bound fill.
- The pattern shows four different boundary scenarios: top edge overflow, top-right corner overflow, left edge overflow, and right/bottom-right edge overflow.
- This is consistent with PTX texture/surface boundary handling modes where out-of-bounds accesses return a fill value rather than wrapping or clamping.


Figure 9 Out of boundary access

#### 5.5.3.4. [`.tile::scatter4` and `.tile::gather4` modes](#tensor-tiled-scatter4-gather4-modes)

These modes are similar to the tiled mode with restriction that these modes work only on 2D tensor data.
`Tile::scatter4` and `Tile::gather4` modes are used to access multiple non-contiguous rows of tensor data.

In `Tile::scatter4` mode single 2D source tensor is divided into four rows in the 2D destination tensor.
In `Tile::gather4` mode four rows in the source 2D tensor are combined to form single 2D destination tensor.

These modes work on four rows and hence the instruction will take:

1. four tensor coordinates across the dimension 0
2. one tensor coordinate across the dimension 1

The interleave layout is not supported for `.tile::scatter4` and `.tile::gather4` modes.

All other constraints and rules of the tile mode apply to these modes as well.

##### 5.5.3.4.1. [Bounding Box](#tensor-tiled-scatter4-gather4-modes-bounding-box)

For `Tile::scatter4` and `Tile::gather4` modes, four request coordinates will form four Bounding
Boxes in the tensor space.

[Figure 10](#tiled-scatter4-gather4-bounding-box) shows an example of the same with start
coordinates (1, 2), (1, 5), (1, 0) and (1, 9).

The size of the bounding box in the dimension 0 represents the length of the rows.
The size of the bounding box in the dimension 1 must be one.





[Figure: tiled-scatter4-gather4-bounding-box]
CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure demonstrates the tiled::scatter4/tiled::gather4 mode bounding box example in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows how four separate 2D regions (tiles) in a 2D grid (representing shared memory layout) are mapped to contiguous addresses in shared memory. Each "Start coordinate" defines the top-left corner of a bounding box region, and the four regions are packed into shared memory at specific addresses.

LEFT SIDE - 2D GRID:
The grid has:
- Horizontal axis labeled "col index" with columns numbered 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (left to right)
- Vertical axis labeled "row index" with rows numbered 0 through 10 (top to bottom)
- The grid cells are mostly green (background), with four distinct colored rectangular regions highlighted

FOUR HIGHLIGHTED REGIONS (bounding boxes):

1. Start coordinate 3 (top region):
   - Located at approximately row 0, columns 1-2
   - Color: Orange/salmon
   - Appears to be a 1-row by 2-column region (row 0, cols 1-2)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 3" label on the left to this region

2. Start coordinate 1 (second region):
   - Located at approximately row 2, columns 1-4
   - Color: Yellow/gold
   - Appears to be a 1-row by 4-column region (row 2, cols 1-4)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 1" label on the left to this region

3. Start coordinate 2 (third region):
   - Located at approximately rows 4-5, columns 1-4
   - Color: Light blue/white
   - Appears to be a 2-row by 4-column region (rows 4-5, cols 1-4)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 2" label on the left to this region

4. Start coordinate 4 (bottom region):
   - Located at approximately rows 9-10, columns 1-4
   - Color: Gray/dark gray
   - Appears to be a 2-row by 4-column region (rows 9-10, cols 1-4)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 4" label on the left to this region

ARROW:
A large blue arrow points from the 2D grid to the right, toward the shared memory representation.

RIGHT SIDE - SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT:
Labeled "Shared Memory" at the bottom.
Column header: "shm address"
The shared memory is shown as a 4-row by 2-column grid of cells, with the following address mapping:

Row 1: address 0x0   - contains two cells colored yellow (left) and orange/salmon (right)
Row 2: address 0x80  - contains two cells colored yellow (left) and light blue (right)
Row 3: address 0x100 - contains two cells colored orange/salmon (left) and light blue (right)
Row 4: address 0x180 - contains two cells colored gray (left) and gray (right)

ADDRESS VALUES IN SHARED MEMORY:
- 0x0   (first row)
- 0x80  (second row, decimal 128)
- 0x100 (third row, decimal 256)
- 0x180 (fourth row, decimal 384)

The address spacing between rows is 0x80 (128 bytes), indicating each row of the packed shared memory region is 128 bytes apart.

DATA FLOW/ORGANIZATION:
The figure illustrates that four non-contiguous, differently-positioned rectangular regions in the 2D source grid (identified by their start coordinates) are gathered/scattered and packed into a contiguous block of shared memory. The colors in the shared memory cells correspond to the colors of the source regions in the 2D grid, showing which source data maps to which shared memory location. The tiled scatter4/gather4 operation handles four tiles simultaneously, packing them into shared memory starting at address 0x0 with 0x80-byte (128-byte) stride between rows.

CONCEPT BEING ILLUSTRATED:
This figure demonstrates the tiled::scatter4/tiled::gather4 mode bounding box example in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows how four separate 2D regions (tiles) in a 2D grid (representing shared memory layout) are mapped to contiguous addresses in shared memory. Each "Start coordinate" defines the top-left corner of a bounding box region, and the four regions are packed into shared memory at specific addresses.

LEFT SIDE - 2D GRID:
The grid has:
- Horizontal axis labeled "col index" with columns numbered 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (left to right)
- Vertical axis labeled "row index" with rows numbered 0 through 10 (top to bottom)
- The grid cells are mostly green (background), with four distinct colored rectangular regions highlighted

FOUR HIGHLIGHTED REGIONS (bounding boxes):

1. Start coordinate 3 (top region):
   - Located at approximately row 0, columns 1-2
   - Color: Orange/salmon
   - Appears to be a 1-row by 2-column region (row 0, cols 1-2)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 3" label on the left to this region

2. Start coordinate 1 (second region):
   - Located at approximately row 2, columns 1-4
   - Color: Yellow/gold
   - Appears to be a 1-row by 4-column region (row 2, cols 1-4)
   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 1" label on the left to this region

   - Appears to be a 2-row by 4-column region (rows 4-5, cols 1-4)

   - Arrow points from "Start coordinate 4" label on the left to this region

A large blue arrow points from the 2D grid to the right, toward the shared memory representation.

RIGHT SIDE - SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT:
Labeled "Shared Memory" at the bottom.
Column header: "shm address"
The shared memory is shown as a 4-row by 2-column grid of cells, with the following address mapping:

Row 1: address 0x0   - contains two cells colored yellow (left) and orange/salmon (right)
Row 2: address 0x80  - contains two cells colored yellow (left) and light blue (right)
Row 3: address 0x100 - contains two cells colored orange/salmon (left) and light blue (right)
Row 4: address 0x180 - contains two cells colored gray (left) and gray (right)

ADDRESS VALUES IN SHARED MEMORY:
- 0x0   (first row)
- 0x80  (second row, decimal 128)
- 0x100 (third row, decimal 256)
- 0x180 (fourth row, decimal 384)

The address spacing between rows is 0x80 (128 bytes), indicating each row of the packed shared memory region is 128 bytes apart.

DATA FLOW/ORGANIZATION:
The figure illustrates that four non-contiguous, differently-positioned rectangular regions in the 2D source grid (identified by their start coordinates) are gathered/scattered and packed into a contiguous block of shared memory. The colors in the shared memory cells correspond to the colors of the source regions in the 2D grid, showing which source data maps to which shared memory location. The tiled scatter4/gather4 operation handles four tiles simultaneously, packing them into shared memory starting at address 0x0 with 0x80-byte (128-byte) stride between rows.


Figure 10 tiled::scatter4/tiled::gather4 mode bounding box example
